title: Headers and source separation

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Headers and source separation; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ headers expose declarations and template or inline definitions under ODR-safe rules, while ordinary external definitions normally reside in one source file.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A class declaration and function prototype live in a guarded header while one .cpp file defines non-inline member functions. Your goal is to create a multi-file function library with correct declarations and definitions. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and range iteration; pf abstraction calls; Function contracts and return values; Compile and link model. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ headers expose declarations and template or inline definitions under ODR-safe rules, while ordinary external definitions normally reside in one source file. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Templates need visible definitions for instantiation, inline variables have special ODR rules, and macros or using directives in headers leak into includers. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Moving any function definition into a header is safe because include guards prevent duplicate definitions at link time.

includes: Separate interface from implementation using self-contained guarded headers.
Create a multi-file function library with correct declarations and definitions.
excludes: Templates need visible definitions for instantiation, inline variables have special ODR rules, and macros or using directives in headers leak into includers.

outcomes: Create a multi-file function library with correct declarations and definitions.

examples.title: Headers and source separation: defined case
examples.situation: A class declaration and function prototype live in a guarded header while one .cpp file defines non-inline member functions.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A class declaration and function prototype live in a guarded header while one .cpp file defines non-inline member functions.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Include guards prevent repeated text in one translation unit; cross-unit definitions must still satisfy inline, template, or one-definition rules.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Include guards prevent repeated text in one translation unit; cross-unit definitions must still satisfy inline, template, or one-definition rules.

examples.title: Headers and source separation: boundary case
examples.situation: A function template definition remains in the header and is identical in every translation unit that instantiates it.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A function template definition remains in the header and is identical in every translation unit that instantiates it.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Templates need visible definitions for instantiation, inline variables have special ODR rules, and macros or using directives in headers leak into includers.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Moving any function definition into a header is safe because include guards prevent duplicate definitions at link time.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Create a multi-file function library with correct declarations and definitions.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Moving any function definition into a header is safe because include guards prevent duplicate definitions at link time.
misconceptions.correction: Include guards prevent repeated text in one translation unit; cross-unit definitions must still satisfy inline, template, or one-definition rules.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Moving any function definition into a header is safe because include guards prevent duplicate definitions at link time.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Headers and source separation: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Templates need visible definitions for instantiation, inline variables have special ODR rules, and macros or using directives in headers leak into includers.
